Koa can load HTML templates similar to Express. Usage is similar but let me log how to set up ejs.
QueryString is a common way to get URL params. Koa's approach is slightly different from Express, so let me log it.
Koa uses a package called koa-router for routing — similar to Express with a few small differences. Let me log it.
This chapter covers ctx — what it is and how to actually use it.
New learning notes. The previous series was Node.js basics — now I'm starting a new framework: Koa.js.
Stop Users Skipping Login With One Express Middleware

We've already covered member registration, login, and how to tell whether someone is logged in. But what if you don't want users jumping straight to the `user` page by typing the URL into the address bar? That's where middleware comes in. In this post I'll show how to gate a whole route behind login with a tiny Express middleware.
Full-Stack Warrior Node.js: Login, Sessions, and Reading Extra Profile Fields (3)

In the last chapter I roughly covered which API to use for login, then talked about adding Flash messages and extending the user fields. This time I want to actually dig into the login flow — how to stash the user's UID and display name in the session, render them on another page, and pull back the extra profile fields we stored in Firebase.
Full-Stack Warrior Node.js: Building a Login Flow with Firebase Auth (Member System Part 2)

In the last post we finished the registration logic, so now it's time to build the login feature. This post walks through Firebase's `signInWithEmailAndPassword`, fleshing out the data you collect during signup, extending user fields with the Firebase Realtime Database, and adding server-side validation with `express-validator`.
Building a Member System in Node.js with Firebase Auth (Part 1)

Time to build a member system. Instead of rolling our own auth from scratch, we'll lean on Firebase's authentication. In this first part we set up an Express app, wire it to Firebase, and build a working signup flow that creates real accounts you can see in the Firebase console.
When using nodemailer in a Node.js project, I kept hitting Invalid login 534-5.7.14. Here's a note on how to fix it.
